s4.name: Scouting America Scouter Code of Conduct

items: Head count, head count, head count: know who is present before, during, and after every transition.
Use the buddy system—two is standard, and three may be used when needed.
A safe camp is no accident: supervise, assess, train, and equip.
Stay engaged: leaders monitor behavior and intervene when necessary.
Be an upstander, not a bystander.
There is no teaching to compare with example.
Before you speak, your example has already spoken.
Do the right thing—even when nobody is watching.
Make the protection of youth a personal priority.
Model the standard you want repeated.
Discipline the behavior, not the person.
Give a positive direction: say what to do, not only what to stop.
Consequences should be predictable, constructive, and connected to the behavior.
Assume positive intent—but still address harmful behavior.
Never promise a consequence you cannot or should not carry out.
Constructive discipline never uses humiliation, ridicule, or isolation.
If a skit or song conflicts with Scout values, take it out.
Kids come first: program decisions begin with youth safety and well-being.
If safety cannot be maintained, adjust or end the activity.
A near miss is a lesson: report it and improve the program.
High quality is a journey: assess, learn, and improve every session.
Listen carefully. Keep accurate notes. Report promptly. Do not confront the alleged abuser.
Tell a child: you did the right thing, it is not your fault, and I will get help.
When physical boundaries are uncertain, choose a brief, public high five.
Stop, assess the facts, and work the problem—do not make a crisis bigger by rushing.
Incident reports should be timely, clear, concise, and factual.
